mechanism: Proposed mechanisms include withanolide-mediated modulation of stress-response signaling and neuroendocrine pathways. These mechanistic findings are preliminary and do not confirm broad clinical benefit across indications.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference:
nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
contraindications: Use caution with prescription medications and chronic conditions; monitor for intolerance.
safetyNotes: Short-term use (up to about 3 months) is often tolerated, but drowsiness and GI effects occur. Rare clinically apparent liver injury has been reported with commercial ashwagandha products. Avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding; use caution with thyroid, autoimmune, or planned surgery contexts.

sources.title: NIH ODS: Ashwagandha Health Professional Fact Sheet

description: Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) is an Ayurvedic shrub used mainly for stress/sleep-related goals. Human trials suggest some preparations may help stress and insomnia symptoms, but findings vary by extract and study quality.
dosage: Clinical studies often use standardized extracts for roughly 2–4 months, but dosing is product-specific and not interchangeable across preparations.

interactions: May interact with sedatives, immunosuppressants, thyroid hormone therapies, anti-seizure medicines, and some diabetes/blood-pressure medicines.
Use caution in people with hormone-sensitive prostate cancer because some preparations may raise testosterone.
preparation: Commercial products commonly use root extract, root powder, or root/leaf extracts; clinical outcomes differ by extract standardization and dose.
region: Native/established in parts of India, Africa, and the Middle East; widely used in South Asian traditional medicine.
therapeuticUses: traditional support for mild digestive comfort
sideEffects: mild gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, or loose stool)
allergic rash or itching in sensitive individuals
